meta.title: Curiosity on Mars
meta.author: John Perritano
meta.focusQuestion: What is the Curiosity rover's mission on Mars, and what has it taught us?

sections.title: Landing on Mars
paragraphs: Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Cupertino, California, September 20, 2012, 7:20 PM. It will be over in the time it takes to hard-boil an egg. That's the only thing scientists know for sure. Some hold their breath. Others bite their fingernails. All hope the strange-looking spacecraft lands safely on Mars.

The spacecraft's name is Curiosity. Its job is to look for evidence of past or current life on Mars. Curiosity is a mechanical rover larger than a golf cart. In fact, it's the largest robotic vehicle that NASA, the United States' space agency, has ever built.

Curiosity hurtles through space at 13,000 miles (20,921 km) per hour as it breaks through the planet's atmosphere. What happens next is a 7-minute plunge to the surface — and the longest 420 seconds in the lives of NASA scientists on the Mars rover team.
Although the Martian atmosphere is thin, it produces enough drag to slow Curiosity to 1,000 miles (1,609 km) per hour. Still, at this speed, the craft is moving far too fast to land.
But scientists have planned for this moment. A 100-pound parachute blossoms from the top of the craft. The chute slows Curiosity to 200 miles (321 km) per hour, the speed of a Formula 1 race car.
Seconds later, the parachute releases. With seconds to go and the surface of Mars fast approaching, the Sky Crane holding Curiosity beneath it fires its rocket engines. They slow the craft even more.

About 66 feet (20 m) above the planet's surface, the Sky Crane gently lowers Curiosity to the ground on cables. Scientists have never before tried to land a spacecraft using this method. Will it work? No one knows. The control room is silent.
Finally, the seven minutes are up. "Touchdown confirmed!" someone shouts. Cheers fill the room. People hug. A few cry. Curiosity has landed.

sections.title: Searching for E.T.
paragraphs: Mars has fascinated people for centuries. The ancient Romans and Greeks named the planet for their gods of war. In 1877, an Italian astronomer wrongly thought he saw canals on the Martian surface. His mistake gave rise to the idea that someone was living there.

As time passed, people soon realized that Mars and Earth had a lot in common. Mars is just as old as Earth. As on Earth, the seasons on Mars change. The planet has volcanoes, mountains, and valleys. Yet, Earth has one thing that has yet to be found on Mars — life.

As far as we know, Earth is the only planet in our solar system that has ever been home to living things. Scientists hope that will all change by the time Curiosity's mission ends in two years. They hope the Red Planet has, or once supported, life.

Although NASA sent the spacecraft to Mars to search for aliens, the rover won't be looking for little green men. Instead, it will search for tiny extraterrestrial life-forms hiding beneath rocks or buried under the soil. Such creatures might be smaller than a one-celled animal or as tiny as a virus.

Where might these critters have come from? Long ago, water flowed on Mars. Water is vital for life. If Mars was wet, perhaps life flowered on its rust-red surface. If life did bloom on Mars, it might have left behind some proof.
Besides Earth, Mars is the most studied planet in our solar system. Humans have sent many space probes to Mars to look for life and study its geology.

The most famous missions involve robotic rovers. In 1996, the United States launched the Pathfinder probe. The probe landed on Mars on July 4, 1997. Two days later, a six-wheeled rover named Sojourner began roaming the Martian surface. Sojourner's findings showed scientists that Mars was more Earthlike than originally thought.

In 2004, two other rovers — Spirit and Opportunity — arrived. They landed on opposite sides of the planet. These robots were more complex than Sojourner. Their mission was supposed to last ninety days. Spirit went silent in 2010, but Opportunity is still on the job.

sections.title: Mobile Science Lab
paragraphs: If there is life on Mars, Curiosity has a good chance of finding it. It is the brainiest Mars rover ever — a science lab on wheels. On August 5, 2012, the rover wasted no time in getting to work after landing. Curiosity popped the protective dust covers off its cameras and turned on its weather station. It took its first images and beamed them back to Earth.
Curiosity is outfitted with many newly designed pieces of equipment, including 3-D cameras. Scientists can now view the Martian landscape as if they were walking on the planet themselves.

Curiosity also has a rock-blasting laser. Once the laser zaps a rock, special instruments examine the gases that spew into the air. The rover also has a drill that can grind bits of boulders into dust. Curiosity can look at the dust and determine the makeup of the minerals and chemicals. The rover can also scoop up and study bits of Martian dirt.

What exactly are Curiosity and the NASA scientists looking for? The rover's mission is to search for the building blocks of life. These include carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, and other elements. If Curiosity finds these elements, it means life may now exist, or in the past have existed, on the Red Planet.

sections.title: Driving Curiosity
paragraphs: Some people drive buses for a living. Others drive taxicabs. Still others drive delivery vans. When Matt Heverly is on the job, he drives Curiosity.

Mars is about twice the size of Earth's moon. Yet, the rover is motoring only in one neighborhood, called Gale Crater. A meteor created the crater about three billion years ago. The hole is the size of Connecticut and Rhode Island combined. Inside the crater is a mountain taller than Mount Rainier, which towers over Seattle, Washington.

Scientists think they have the best opportunity to find evidence of life inside this crater. That's because scientists believe Gale Crater was once flooded with water. As a result, the crater is packed with clays and sulfates — types of minerals. These minerals are byproducts of water. Curiosity will study the soil to see if any microbes are hiding inside.

Driving Curiosity is a hard job; one bad turn could doom the mission. That's why Heverly meets with scientists to talk about where the rover should go before he gets behind the wheel or, in this case, the computer.

Once in the driver's seat, Heverly must put on special glasses to study the 3-D images that Curiosity sends back. He then uses a computer program that simulates the route. Heverly enters hundreds of computer commands that steer the craft. Curiosity crawled only thirty feet a day when it first landed.

sections.title: Listening In
paragraphs: Talking to Curiosity is more complicated than picking up a smartphone and calling a friend. The rover can send messages directly to Earth. It can also relay signals by using three satellites orbiting Mars.

Scientists send and receive the signals using a system of giant antenna dishes. The system is called the Deep Space Network. The huge dishes are located near Madrid, Spain; Canberra, Australia; and the Mojave Desert in California.
Scientists will be listening — at least for the next two years — as Curiosity drives along the Martian landscape, hoping to see if anyone else is out there.

vocabulary.definition: something that supports a theory or claim
vocabulary.sentence: Its job is to look for evidence of past or current life on Mars.

options: a type of robotic vehicle used in space
something that supports a theory or claim
a thin layer of gas around a planet

vocabulary.definition: existing or coming from outside Earth or its atmosphere
vocabulary.sentence: It will search for tiny extraterrestrial life-forms hiding beneath rocks or buried under the soil.

options: existing or coming from outside Earth

vocabulary.definition: of or relating to the planet Mars
vocabulary.sentence: Although the Martian atmosphere is thin, it produces enough drag to slow Curiosity.

options: related to the Roman god of war
of or relating to the planet Mars

vocabulary.sentence: Curiosity will study the soil to see if any microbes are hiding inside.

options: types of minerals found in soil

vocabulary.definition: a set purpose for doing something; a special task or assignment
vocabulary.sentence: Their mission was supposed to last ninety days.

options: a set purpose or special task

vocabulary.definition: a vehicle used to explore the surface of an object in space
vocabulary.sentence: Curiosity is a mechanical rover larger than a golf cart.

options: a satellite that orbits a planet
a vehicle used to explore a planet's surface
a crater on the surface of Mars

vocabulary.definition: substances made of one type of atom that cannot be broken down into simpler substances
vocabulary.sentence: These include carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, and other elements.

options: substances made of one type of atom
gases found only in the atmosphere

vocabulary.definition: a device that projects intense, focused light of similar wavelengths
vocabulary.sentence: Curiosity also has a rock-blasting laser.

options: a drill that grinds rocks into dust
a device that projects intense, focused light

vocabulary.definition: to pass along something, such as information or a signal
vocabulary.sentence: It can also relay signals by using three satellites orbiting Mars.

options: to block signals from reaching Earth
to receive but not send information
to store data for future use
to pass along something, such as a signal

vocabulary.definition: of or related to a device that is programmed to perform tasks for humans
vocabulary.sentence: Curiosity is the largest robotic vehicle that NASA has ever built.

options: controlled by remote signals from Earth
of or related to a programmed device that performs tasks
made of metal and able to move on its own
designed to look like a human

vocabulary.definition: a natural or human-made object that orbits Earth or another object in space
vocabulary.sentence: It can also relay signals by using three satellites orbiting Mars.

options: objects that orbit a planet or other body in space
spacecraft that land on planetary surfaces
dishes that receive signals from Earth

vocabulary.definition: models or imitates the appearance or condition of something
vocabulary.sentence: He then uses a computer program that simulates the route.

options: to create a real version of something

questions.question: What is Curiosity's main job on Mars?
questions.evidence: The spacecraft's name is Curiosity. Its job is to look for evidence of past or current life on Mars.
choices: To test whether a Sky Crane can land safely on Mars
To look for evidence of past or current life on Mars
To study the weather and atmosphere of Mars
To build a base for future human astronauts
questions.explanation: The text directly states that Curiosity's job is to look for evidence of past or current life on Mars.

questions.question: How did Curiosity finally land on the surface of Mars?
questions.evidence: About 66 feet (20 m) above the planet's surface, the Sky Crane gently lowers Curiosity to the ground on cables.
choices: It landed on wheels like a regular spacecraft
A parachute slowed it all the way to the ground
A Sky Crane lowered it on cables from above
Scientists guided it by remote control from Earth
questions.explanation: After the parachute and Sky Crane rockets slowed it, the Sky Crane lowered Curiosity on cables to the surface — a method never tried before by NASA.

questions.question: What caused people in New York City to panic on the night of October 30, 1938?
questions.evidence: They heard something shocking. "A huge flaming object" had smashed into a New Jersey farm. The Martians were invading! The broadcast was a radio play called "The War of the Worlds."
choices: An astronomer announced he saw canals on Mars
NASA reported that a spacecraft had crashed on Mars
They heard a radio play about Martians invading Earth
Newspapers reported that a meteor had landed in New York
questions.explanation: People heard Orson Welles's radio play "The War of the Worlds" and thought the Martian invasion was real. Many missed the disclaimer at the beginning.

questions.question: What does the word "microbes" mean?
questions.evidence: Curiosity will study the soil to see if any microbes are hiding inside. (Glossary: microbes — microscopic organisms)
choices: Types of minerals found in Martian rocks
Tiny robots designed to explore small spaces
Pieces of ice that may contain ancient bacteria
Microscopic organisms
questions.explanation: The glossary defines microbes as microscopic organisms. These are the tiny life-forms Curiosity hopes to find in Martian soil.

questions.question: What do Sojourner, Spirit, Opportunity, and Curiosity have in common?
questions.evidence: The most famous missions involve robotic rovers. In 1996, the United States launched the Pathfinder probe... Sojourner began roaming... In 2004, two other rovers — Spirit and Opportunity — arrived. ... Curiosity is the brainiest Mars rover ever.
choices: They are all robotic rovers that have studied the surface of Mars
They were all built to last exactly ninety days on Mars
They all found clear evidence of life on Mars
They are still actively working on Mars today
questions.explanation: All four are robotic rovers sent to study the Martian surface. Sojourner (1997), Spirit and Opportunity (2004), and Curiosity (2012) each contributed to our understanding of Mars.

questions.question: Why are scientists especially interested in Gale Crater?
questions.evidence: Scientists think they have the best opportunity to find evidence of life inside this crater. That's because scientists believe Gale Crater was once flooded with water. As a result, the crater is packed with clays and sulfates — types of minerals. These minerals are byproducts of water.
choices: Because it contains the tallest mountain on Mars
Because Gale Crater was once flooded with water, and water is vital for life
Because it is the largest crater in the solar system
Because the soil there is easier for the rover to drive on
questions.explanation: Scientists believe Gale Crater was once flooded with water. The minerals found there (clays and sulfates) are byproducts of water — and water is vital for life.

questions.question: How is Earth different from Mars, according to the text?
questions.evidence: Mars is just as old as Earth. As on Earth, the seasons on Mars change. The planet has volcanoes, mountains, and valleys. Yet, Earth has one thing that has yet to be found on Mars — life.
choices: Earth has volcanoes and mountains, but Mars does not
Earth is much younger than Mars
Life is known to exist on Earth but has not been found on Mars
Earth's seasons do not change like Mars's seasons do
questions.explanation: The text says Earth and Mars have a lot in common — same age, changing seasons, volcanoes, mountains, valleys. The one difference: Earth has life, and Mars has not been found to have life yet.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is an opinion rather than a fact?
questions.evidence: Facts can be proven true or false. Opinions express beliefs or feelings. "Curiosity is the brainiest Mars rover ever" and "Exploring Mars is the most exciting operation NASA has ever undertaken" are opinions because they express judgment.
choices: Curiosity landed on Mars on August 5, 2012
Curiosity has a rock-blasting laser that zaps rocks
The building blocks of life include carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen
Exploring Mars is the most exciting operation NASA has ever undertaken
questions.explanation: "The most exciting operation" is a judgment or belief — it's an opinion. The other three are facts that can be verified.

questions.question: What is the author's main purpose in writing this book?
questions.evidence: The book presents factual information about Curiosity's mission, equipment, and findings on Mars. It uses scientific data, dates, and measurements throughout.
choices: To entertain readers with an exciting story about space
To inform readers about the Curiosity rover's mission on Mars
To persuade readers that NASA should send more missions to Mars
To explain the step-by-step process of how rockets launch
questions.explanation: This is an informational nonfiction text. The author's main purpose is to inform readers about Curiosity — what it is, what it does, and what it has discovered on Mars.

questions.question: Why can't scientists just use a smartphone to talk to Curiosity?
questions.evidence: Talking to Curiosity is more complicated than picking up a smartphone and calling a friend. The rover can send messages directly to Earth. It can also relay signals by using three satellites orbiting Mars. Scientists send and receive the signals using a system of giant antenna dishes.
choices: Because communicating with Mars requires satellites and giant antenna dishes across the globe
Because Mars is too far away for any signals to reach
Because Curiosity's equipment is too old to connect to phones
Because NASA has not yet developed the technology needed
questions.explanation: Communicating with Curiosity requires relaying signals through satellites orbiting Mars and receiving them with giant antenna dishes (the Deep Space Network) located around the world — much more complex than a phone call.

questions.question: Do you think Curiosity's mission is worth the billions of dollars it costs? Why or why not? Use evidence from the text to support your answer.
questions.explanation: This is an opinion question. Support your answer with reasons and evidence from the text — for example, what Curiosity has found or might find, and whether that knowledge is valuable.
opinionPrompts: What has Curiosity discovered or might discover on Mars?
How does this knowledge benefit people on Earth?
What evidence from the text supports your opinion?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: NASA scientists used an innovative Sky Crane method to safely land the Curiosity rover on Mars in a dramatic seven-minute descent.
keyDetails: Curiosity is the largest robotic vehicle NASA has ever built
The descent took only seven minutes — the time to hard-boil an egg
A parachute, Sky Crane rockets, and cables slowed and lowered the rover
Scientists had never tried this landing method before
distractors: Curiosity searches for the building blocks of life
Matt Heverly drives Curiosity using 3-D images

summarizeActivities.sectionTitle: Searching for E.T. and Previous Rovers
summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Mars and Earth have a lot in common, but only Earth has known life. Scientists hope Curiosity will find evidence that Mars once had life, building on the work of previous rovers.
keyDetails: Mars has volcanoes, mountains, valleys, and changing seasons like Earth
Curiosity searches for tiny extraterrestrial life-forms, not aliens
Water once flowed on Mars, and water is vital for life
Previous rovers — Sojourner, Spirit, and Opportunity — all studied Mars
distractors: The Sky Crane lowered Curiosity on cables
Gale Crater was once flooded with water

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Curiosity is a mobile science laboratory equipped with advanced tools — including 3-D cameras, a laser, and a drill — to search for the building blocks of life on Mars.
keyDetails: Curiosity is the brainiest Mars rover ever — a science lab on wheels
It has 3-D cameras so scientists can view the landscape as if walking on Mars
A rock-blasting laser and drill analyze the makeup of rocks and soil
The rover looks for elements like carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, and oxygen
distractors: The Sky Crane landing method was tried for the first time
Deep Space Network dishes are located in three countries

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Matt Heverly drives Curiosity only within Gale Crater because scientists believe the crater was once flooded with water and may hold evidence of past life.
keyDetails: Gale Crater was created by a meteor about three billion years ago
The crater is packed with clays and sulfates — minerals that are byproducts of water
Heverly uses special 3-D glasses and a computer program to simulate the route
Curiosity crawled only thirty feet a day when it first landed
distractors: Curiosity beamed its first images back to Earth after landing
Signals are relayed through three satellites orbiting Mars

landing.title: Evidence
landing.content: Evidence = proof that supports a claim or theory. Curiosity's whole mission is to look for EVIDENCE of life on Mars!

landing.title: Cause & Effect
landing.content: Cause: Thin Martian atmosphere creates drag. Effect: Curiosity slows from 13,000 mph to 1,000 mph. But that's still too fast!

landing.title: 7 Minutes of Terror
landing.content: Entry → parachute → Sky Crane → touchdown. Each step had to work perfectly. Scientists couldn't control it — they just had to wait and watch!

searchingET.title: Extraterrestrial
searchingET.content: Extra = outside, terrestrial = Earth. Extraterrestrial = from OUTSIDE Earth! Scientists search for tiny life-forms, NOT little green men.

searchingET.title: Earth vs. Mars
searchingET.content: Same: age, seasons, volcanoes, mountains, valleys. Different: Earth has LIFE. Mars has NOT been found to have life — YET.

searchingET.title: Rover Timeline
searchingET.content: 1997: Sojourner → 2004: Spirit & Opportunity → 2012: Curiosity. Each rover was bigger and more complex than the last!

mobileLab.title: Elements
mobileLab.content: Elements = building blocks of matter. Carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen — these are the elements needed for LIFE!

mobileLab.title: Laser
mobileLab.content: Curiosity's laser ZAPS rocks → instruments examine the gases → determines what minerals and chemicals the rock contains.

mobileLab.title: Main Idea & Details
mobileLab.content: Main idea: Curiosity is a science lab on wheels. Details: 3-D cameras, rock-blasting laser, drill, soil scoop — all looking for building blocks of life!

driving.title: Microbes
driving.content: Micro = tiny, be = life. Microbes = microscopic organisms! Curiosity looks for these tiny life-forms in Gale Crater's soil.

driving.title: Why Gale Crater?
driving.content: Cause: Gale Crater was once flooded with water. Effect: It's packed with clays and sulfates (byproducts of water). So life might be hiding there!

driving.title: Driving is Hard!
driving.content: Matt Heverly must: 1) Meet with scientists, 2) Wear special 3-D glasses, 3) Use a computer to simulate the route, 4) Enter hundreds of commands. One bad turn = doom!

listening.title: Relay & Satellites
listening.content: Relay = pass along. Satellites = objects orbiting Mars. Curiosity relays signals through 3 satellites → giant antenna dishes on Earth.

listening.title: Deep Space Network
listening.content: 3 giant antenna dish locations: Madrid (Spain), Canberra (Australia), Mojave Desert (California). They're spread around the world so Mars is always in range!

grammar.title: Prepositional Phrases
grammar.explanation: A preposition shows the relationship between things — WHERE, WHEN, or HOW something happens. A prepositional phrase starts with a preposition and ends with a noun. Example: "above the planet's surface" tells us WHERE the Sky Crane is.
rules: Common prepositions: above, beneath, on, under, through, into, over, across, near, from, to, around, between, during, with
A prepositional phrase = preposition + noun/pronoun: "on cables", "through space", "beneath rocks"
Prepositional phrases tell WHERE (location), WHEN (time), or HOW (manner) something happens
The same preposition can have different meanings in different contexts: "on Mars" (WHERE) vs. "on August 5" (WHEN)

wordBuilding.answer: beneath rocks or buried under the soil

exercises.sentence: About 66 feet above the planet's surface, the Sky Crane gently lowers Curiosity to the ground on cables.
exercises.hint: Find the prepositional phrases that tell WHERE Curiosity is lowered. (There are 3!)

exercises.sentence: It will search for tiny extraterrestrial life-forms hiding beneath rocks or buried under the soil.
exercises.hint: Where might life-forms be hiding? Find the phrases that tell WHERE.

exercises.sentence: Curiosity hurtles through space at 13,000 miles per hour as it breaks through the planet's atmosphere.
exercises.hint: WHERE is Curiosity hurtling? HOW FAST?

exercises.sentence: Mars has fascinated people for centuries.
exercises.hint: WHEN has Mars fascinated people? Find the phrase that tells HOW LONG.

exercises.question: Which is a prepositional phrase?
exercises.sentence: Scientists have never before tried to land a spacecraft using this method.
exercises.explanation: "to land" starts with the preposition "to" and "a spacecraft" is the noun — together they form a prepositional phrase.

exercises.question: What does the prepositional phrase "in the control room" tell us?
exercises.explanation: "In the control room" tells us WHERE — it describes the location of the scientists.

exercises.sentence: The Sky Crane holding Curiosity _____ it fires its rocket engines.
exercises.explanation: Curiosity is hanging BELOW the Sky Crane, so "beneath" is correct: "the Sky Crane holding Curiosity beneath it".

exercises.sentence: Curiosity is outfitted _____ many newly designed pieces of equipment.
exercises.explanation: "Outfitted with" means "equipped with" — Curiosity has many new tools.

exercises.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "hurtle"?
exercises.explanation: "Hurtle" means to move at very high speed — Curiosity hurtles through space at 13,000 mph!

exercises.prompt: Which word is a SYNONYM of "vital"?
exercises.explanation: "Vital" means absolutely necessary or essential. Water is vital for life!

exercises.prompt: Which word is an ANTONYM (opposite) of "silent"?
exercises.explanation: "Silent" means no sound. The opposite is "noisy" — full of sound.

exercises.prompt: Which word is an ANTONYM (opposite) of "peaceful"?
exercises.explanation: "Peaceful" means calm and quiet. The opposite is "violent" — full of force and destruction.
